title: Document tree and DOM concept

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents draft for Document tree and DOM concept; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: HTML Living Standard; living standard accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Document tree and DOM concept
sources.claim: The DOM is the browser's object-tree representation of the parsed document, not the original source text itself.

canonicalExplanation.summary: The DOM is the browser's object-tree representation of the parsed document, not the original source text itself.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A missing table wrapper is inserted by parsing rules, so the inspected DOM contains nodes absent from typed source. Your goal is to draw or inspect the element tree for a supplied document and diagnose a parser repair. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Elements, attributes and nesting. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: The DOM is the browser's object-tree representation of the parsed document, not the original source text itself. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Parser repair, script mutation, templates, and developer tools can make the live DOM differ from view-source text. This draft therefore rejects: The DOM and the saved HTML file are always byte-for-byte identical.

includes: Relate nested source elements to parent, child and sibling nodes exposed to browser tools.
Draw or inspect the element tree for a supplied document and diagnose a parser repair.
excludes: Parser repair, script mutation, templates, and developer tools can make the live DOM differ from view-source text.

outcomes: Draw or inspect the element tree for a supplied document and diagnose a parser repair.

examples.title: Document tree and DOM concept: concrete case
examples.situation: A missing table wrapper is inserted by parsing rules, so the inspected DOM contains nodes absent from typed source.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A missing table wrapper is inserted by parsing rules, so the inspected DOM contains nodes absent from typed source.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Treat the DOM as the live parsed tree; inspect it when reasoning about structure because it can differ from source text.
